# Questions in Livestream 30

Live Stream #30: Full Moon on Saturday.

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction |
|---:|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=244" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:04</a> | How did bulls become the go-to sacrifice in the ancient Near East, and could this connect to a Proto-Indo-European creation myth? | He said bulls mattered because cattle were economically central, so sacrificing one meant surrendering something valuable. He did not see a likely Proto-Indo-European connection because that myth probably came later. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=395" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">6:35</a> | How should claims about Canaanites in Egypt and Hoffmeier's motives be evaluated? | He said motive attacks are a distraction. The real issue is the historical connection between Canaanites in Avaris, the Exodus tradition, and later Israel in Canaan. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=705" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">11:45</a> | Does Jephthah's "300 years" statement in Judges 11 help date the Exodus? | He said not much. Because Jephthah is presented as an unreliable speaker in that passage, the number is weak evidence for Exodus chronology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=1166" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">19:26</a> | What are his thoughts on Sargon of Akkad? | He rejected the idea that Moses is copied from Sargon, but said Sargon's story is important for river-culture child-abandonment traditions and as an archetype of the powerful usurper. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=1332" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">22:12</a> | How does he interpret the 430 years in Exodus 12:40 and Galatians 3:17, and does he favor a short or long sojourn? | He favored the short-sojourn reading, arguing that the Septuagint and Samaritan Pentateuch support counting time in both Canaan and Egypt. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=1464" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">24:24</a> | Could Chedorlaomer be identified with a known historical king? | Possibly, but he said the identification is uncertain because more than one candidate name fits the relevant window. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=1559" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">25:59</a> | How should Exodus 21 on slaves, beatings, and "property" be understood? | He said the passage still reflects slavery, but also that Israelite law placed unusual limits on slaveowners compared with surrounding cultures and should not be flattened into later chattel-slavery assumptions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=1910" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">31:50</a> | What stands out about the Ain Dara temple? | He said the most memorable feature is the giant footprints at the entrance, a symbolic way of showing the deity entering the temple. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=2043" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:03</a> | Did Solomon repent of idolatry and lust before his death? | He said scripture is not explicit, so certainty is impossible, but he leaned toward seeing Solomon as still among the righteous despite ending badly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=2370" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">39:30</a> | Does Exodus 3:6 show continued worship of God in Avaris similar to the patriarchs? | He said there is no evidence of Yahweh worship at Avaris; the phrase "God of your father" may refer to an ancestor such as Levi rather than proving continuity there. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=2583" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">43:03</a> | How long did Pithom last compared with Pi-Ramesses, and have ruins been found in the Wadi Tumilat? | He said Pithom is at Tell el-Retaba, lasted longer than Pi-Ramesses into the Third Intermediate Period, and is still producing excavation evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=2730" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">45:30</a> | Who was the queen of Sheba, where was Sheba, and was she related to Punt? | He said Punt was in eastern Africa, Sheba/Saba was in the Arabian peninsula, and the queen was likely acting as an ambassador in trade negotiations with Solomon. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=2889" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">48:09</a> | Could Avaris have been named after the biblical Joseph? | He rejected the etymology, saying the name does not work linguistically and Avaris was already an established Middle Kingdom city before Joseph's time. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=3044" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">50:44</a> | What is the Near Eastern context of Genesis 38:8-10? | He explained it through levirate marriage: Onan's sin was refusing to preserve his brother's line and thereby violating clan integrity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=3283" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">54:43</a> | What were forges and kilns fired with if wood was so expensive? | He said Egyptians could use reeds, chaff, dung, scraps, and wood from controlled regions such as Sinai or Kush, while large timber remained costly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=3486" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">58:06</a> | Does he think Ramesses III was assassinated? | Yes. He cited the harem conspiracy evidence and the mummy's throat wound as reasons he thinks assassination is now effectively settled. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=3627" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:00:27</a> | Could characters in Homer's Iliad be tribes the way Adam was a tribe? | He said he did not know Greek culture well enough to answer confidently, but thought Greek polity looked less tribal than ancient Near Eastern clan structures. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=3755" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:02:35</a> | How does ancient Near Eastern temple design compare with early Catholic or Orthodox basilica design? | He said he could speak mainly from Egyptian examples, where early church layouts seem more Roman or Hellenistic than Jewish or broadly Near Eastern. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=4007" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:06:47</a> | Was there already water in the rock that God commanded Moses to speak to in Numbers 20:11? | He thought the water was probably already there as a spring, but God intended Moses to reveal it in a way that displayed God's goodness rather than Moses' anger. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=4203" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:10:03</a> | Are there good books on the history of the modern Messianic Jewish movement, and does it date directly to first-century Jewish Christians? | He did not have a book recommendation and said modern Messianic Judaism is not a direct institutional descendant, though Jews have converted to Christianity throughout church history. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=4426" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:13:46</a> | Did demonic possession appear suddenly with Christ, or was there a concept of possession before Christ? | He said possession did not appear suddenly; it is found in Second Temple literature before Christ and continued in early Christian ministry after Christ. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=4588" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:16:28</a> | What about the theory that Moses was assassinated because he failed to enter the promised land, and why is his burial place unknown? | He dismissed the assassination theory as unsupported and said the unknown grave likely prevented veneration and ancestor-worship problems. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=4812" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:20:12</a> | Does Deuteronomy 1:37 contradict Numbers 20:11 on why Moses could not enter the promised land? | He said no; after checking the sequence, he connected the broader Deuteronomy statement with Numbers 32 and treated Moses' own exclusion as already grounded in Numbers 20. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=5283" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:28:03</a> | Did ancient people cultivate forestry to secure wood resources, or did military activity damage long-term agricultural endeavors? | He said ancient Egypt does not appear to have practiced silviculture in that way, and exploitation of forest resources likely damaged some areas long term. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=5558" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:32:38</a> | Which language is closer to Middle Egyptian, modern Coptic or Nubian? | He said modern Coptic is closest, though it is nearly dead as a living language and differs in orthography and its own later features. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=5644" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:34:04</a> | Would he debate Dr. James White on autonomy and hermeneutics related to Exodus 9? | Probably not. He said he generally finds debates unproductive and would rather teach and do research unless a debate serves a very specific purpose. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=5868" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:37:48</a> | What does he think of Mark S. Smith's argument that Deuteronomy 32:8-9 shows Old Testament religion was polytheistic? | He rejected the argument, saying the literary structure reads as God dividing the nations while reserving Israel for himself, not as Elyon giving Israel to a separate Yahweh. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=6302" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:45:02</a> | Did the Israelites have board games, or were they too busy surviving? | He said probably yes, citing Levantine evidence such as the Megiddo ivories, game boards, dice, and throwing sticks. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=6551" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:49:11</a> | If a creationist is someone who believes Yahweh created the world, how do his views differ from old-earth creationism? | He said creationism is usually more specifically about creation ex nihilo, but he currently does not identify with young-earth, old-earth, evolutionary, or theistic-evolutionary positions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=7041" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:57:21</a> | Is there a book he would recommend for biblical geography, and would he sign a copy of his book? | He recommended Barry Beitzel's atlas and another atlas by Alan Millard and Paul Lawrence, and said he would be happy to sign the book by arrangement. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/CG3UQpEmAsk?t=7219" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:00:19</a> | What is his favorite archaeological find, and what does he hope gets found in his lifetime? | His favorite was Tutankhamun's tomb. He hoped for finds such as the tomb of Imhotep, Sukkot, Pi-hahiroth, Baal-zephon, or a plausible tomb of Joseph. |
